# Reproducible reporting with Jupyter notebooks

We introduce Jupyter notebooks as a way to combine code, output, and written explanation in a single document, and walk through a prepared notebook that puts together several skills from this course into a small analysis.

{{< video https://vimeo.com/1196320606/3acd0a68f8?fl=ml&fe=ec >}}

The notebook used in the video is available on GitHub: [skl401-notebook.ipynb](https://github.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/blob/master/skl401-notebook.ipynb). Open the page and use the download button there to save it to your computer.

::: {.callout-note}
The notebook loads its data with `pd.read_csv("data/gapminder.csv")`, a relative path. For this to work, place the notebook file in the root of your project folder, alongside a `data` subfolder that contains `gapminder.csv`.
:::

## Exercises

### Exercise 5.5.1

Start a new Jupyter notebook. You will build it up progressively across the exercises on this page, ending with a small finished analysis.

1. Add a markdown cell at the top with a title (`# Health Spending and Life Expectancy`) and one sentence describing what the notebook explores: how health spending and life expectancy compare across six countries, using the `healthexp` dataset.
2. Add a code cell below it that imports `pandas`, `seaborn`, and `matplotlib.pyplot`, loads `healthexp` with `sns.load_dataset()`, and prints its shape.
3. Run both cells. Confirm the shape appears directly below the code cell, and that the markdown cell renders as formatted text rather than plain code.

---

### Exercise 5.5.2

Continue the same notebook by adding a summary of the data.

1. Add a markdown cell with a short header introducing a question: how does health spending compare across countries?
2. Add a code cell below it that groups `healthexp` by `Country`, computes the mean `Spending_USD` and mean `Life_Expectancy` for each, rounds to 1 decimal place, resets the index, and sorts by mean spending in descending
order. Assign the result to `summary`.
3. Run the cell and confirm the table appears as output directly below the code.

---

### Exercise 5.5.3

Add a "dynamic sentence" to the notebook — a sentence that inserts an actual computed value from your data, rather than a number you typed in by hand.

1. Add a code cell that extracts the country name and mean spending value from the first row of `summary` (the highest-spending country).
2. In the same cell, use an f-string inside a `print()` statement to display a sentence such as: `"The country with the highest average health spending is USA, at $4388.6 per capita."` — but built from the variables you just
extracted, not typed as a fixed string.

---

### Exercise 5.5.4

Add a chart to visualize the comparison from exercise 5.5.2.

1. Add a markdown cell with a short line introducing the chart: a bar chart will make the differences across countries easier to see than the table alone.
2. Add a code cell that creates a horizontal bar chart of `summary`, with country on the y-axis and mean spending on the x-axis, using `figsize=(8, 5)` and a colour of your choice. Add an axis label and a title.
3. Run the cell and confirm the chart appears as output directly below the code, in the same way the table did in exercise 5.5.2.

---

### Exercise 5.5.5

Export the finished notebook to a format you could share with someone who does not have Python installed.

1. In Positron, find the notebook export option in the notebook toolbar and export your notebook to HTML. (If you'd rather do this from a terminal: it requires a package called `nbconvert`, which isn't installed by default in this course, so run `pip install nbconvert` once, then `python -m nbconvert --to html your-notebook.ipynb` — on a Mac, use `python3` instead of `python`.)
2. Open the resulting HTML file in a browser. Does it look the same as the notebook?
3. Try clicking on one of the code cells in the exported HTML file. Can you edit it and re-run it? What does this tell you about the difference between the notebook file itself and an exported copy of it?

---

A complete, worked version of this notebook — with all five exercises finished and every cell executed — is available on GitHub: [05-05-exercise-solution.ipynb](https://github.com/isabelhovdahl/skl401/blob/master/05-05-exercise-solution.ipynb). Open the page and use the download button there to save it to your computer.
